A 37-year-old man from Dunmow, who was arrested on suspicion of being in charge of a dog dangerously out of control and causing injury resulting in death, has been bailed until July while inquiries continue, Essex police said on Sunday. The canine, which was a family pet and believed to be a lurcher crossbreed, was seized and tests are under way to formally establish the dog’s breed, the force added. Assistant chief constable Stuart Hooper said: “Our thoughts remain with all those who knew and loved Jamie-Lea. Her young life has been so tragically cut short. “Our detectives are continuing to work around the clock to establish exactly what happened and specialist officers are continuing to support Jamie-Lea’s family. “This is unimaginable for her loved ones and friends and, as such, I would ask people to respect their grief and privacy at this extremely difficult time. “Our officers remain at the scene and anyone with concerns or information can speak with them there or contact us in the usual way.” A post-mortem examination is due to take place on Sunday, police said. lead_story.title: Trump's blockade threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
lead_story.summary: Donald Trump’s threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz has heightened the chance of escalation, but it has not solved the central problem: how far Iran can sustain pressure and how long Washington is willing to bear the economic and military costs of confrontation.
lead_story.source_signal_title: Trump's blockade threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
body: Donald Trump's threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz has increased the chance of a wider confrontation, but it has not altered the basic arithmetic of the crisis. Iran is still trying to show it can absorb pressure, while Washington is weighing how long it can sustain military, diplomatic and economic costs without a clear route to a lasting settlement.

The strait is one of the world's most sensitive waterways because a large share of seaborne oil and gas passes through it. Even the prospect of a blockade or prolonged disruption is enough to unsettle shipping, rattle energy markets and prompt governments and companies to plan for higher costs and supply delays.

For Trump, the blockade threat is a way of signalling resolve after talks with Iran faltered. But threats of this kind also narrow the room for manoeuvre, because they can force both sides to demonstrate strength rather than compromise, making a miscalculation more likely.

Iran's leaders, meanwhile, can seek to exploit the pressure by showing that they can still impose pain on global trade. That may include disruption, delays or coercive measures short of an outright closure, any of which could keep markets nervous without resolving the underlying dispute.

The central problem is that neither side has yet found a durable off-ramp. Washington wants to stop Iran from using the crisis to gain leverage, while Tehran wants to avoid appearing to yield under fire. That leaves the conflict suspended between escalation and uneasy containment.

Unless there is a renewed diplomatic channel, the most likely outcome is continued tension: enough military risk to keep shipping and energy markets on edge, but not enough clarity to end the standoff. That is precisely what makes the situation so dangerous for the region and for economies far beyond it.

stories.title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
stories.summary: An explainer on the mechanics and limits of blockading one of the world’s most strategically sensitive waterways.
stories.source_signal_title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
body: A naval blockade is an attempt to stop ships entering or leaving a designated area, usually by deploying warships, patrol aircraft and surveillance systems to intercept or warn vessels away. In the Strait of Hormuz, that would mean policing a narrow and heavily trafficked passage through which much of the Gulf's oil and gas moves.

In practice, a blockade can take several forms. A navy may seek to halt traffic completely, selectively inspect vessels, or create such a level of risk that shipping companies avoid the route altogether. The latter can be almost as disruptive as a formal closure, even if no declaration of war is made.

The strait is difficult to control because it is shared by multiple states, watched by international forces and used by commercial shipping from across the world. Any attempt to stop traffic would raise immediate legal, military and diplomatic questions, including who has authority to enforce it and how neutral vessels would be treated.

That is why talk of a blockade matters even before a single ship is stopped. The economic effect can begin with uncertainty: insurers raise premiums, companies reroute cargoes and energy markets price in disruption. The result is that a blockade can create damage long before it becomes a fully functioning military reality.

stories.title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
stories.summary: The negotiations ended without a breakthrough, leaving the ceasefire fragile and the region exposed to further escalation or a costly, uneasy pause.
stories.source_signal_title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
body: The talks ended without a breakthrough, leaving the ceasefire fragile and the wider confrontation unresolved. For now, both sides appear to have stepped back from immediate escalation, but neither has agreed the terms that would make the truce durable.

That leaves a familiar but unstable pattern: a pause that reduces the risk of instant conflict, while preserving the grievances that caused the crisis in the first place. Iran will want proof that pressure has eased; the US will want assurances that Tehran cannot simply wait out the moment and resume leverage later.

The danger is that this becomes a prolonged holding pattern, with enough restraint to avoid all-out war but not enough trust to produce a real settlement. In that scenario, shipping, energy prices and regional security would remain vulnerable to sudden shocks whenever either side tests the other's resolve.

stories.title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
stories.summary: Both sides say the other has broken the truce repeatedly, underscoring how little trust remains and how quickly violence could resume.
stories.source_signal_title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
body: Ukraine and Russia have accused each other of carrying out hundreds of breaches of a ceasefire, underlining how little confidence remains between them. Both sides say the other is responsible for repeated attacks, with Kyiv warning that it would respond symmetrically if struck again.

The claims are important because ceasefires often fail first at the edges, through artillery fire, drone attacks or local skirmishes that then spiral into wider violence. Even when a truce formally exists, the absence of trust can make every incident politically explosive.

For civilians near the front line, the accusations are a sign that calm could prove temporary. For diplomats, they are a reminder that a ceasefire without verification, enforcement and a political process behind it can quickly become little more than a pause in fighting.

stories.title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
stories.summary: Reports that a Nigerian air force strike hit a village market while targeting militants are under scrutiny, with the military yet to address them fully.
stories.source_signal_title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
body: Civilians are feared to have been killed after reports that a Nigerian air force strike hit a village market while targeting militants. The military has not yet fully addressed the claims, and the circumstances of the incident remain disputed.

If confirmed, the strike would add to long-running concerns about the conduct of counter-insurgency operations in north-eastern Nigeria, where civilian areas can lie close to militant strongholds and intelligence failures have proved deadly before. Such incidents also deepen mistrust between local communities and the security forces sent to protect them.

The immediate priority is to establish what happened, how many people were affected and whether the attack complied with the rules governing military action. Until that is clear, the episode will sit within a broader pattern of civilian risk in a conflict that has already caused enormous displacement and loss of life.

stories.title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
stories.summary: A fatal crush at a crowded tourist event has again exposed the risks of weak crowd management and emergency response at major public gatherings.
stories.source_signal_title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
body: At least 30 people are feared dead after a crush at a crowded tourist event in Haiti, according to officials. The gathering reportedly drew many young people, and the scale of the incident has raised concern that the emergency response was overwhelmed.

Haiti has long struggled with weak public services, limited crowd control capacity and fragile transport and medical infrastructure, all of which can turn a large public event into a disaster. When people are packed tightly into confined spaces, even a brief panic can lead to catastrophic consequences.

The immediate questions are how the crowd became trapped, whether organisers had adequate safety plans and how quickly injured people could be reached. The incident is likely to renew scrutiny of public event planning in a country where many basic state functions are under severe strain.

stories.title: Asha Bhosle: The sound of Bollywood dies aged 92
stories.summary: The death of the celebrated Indian singer closes a major chapter in South Asian popular culture, with her voice spanning generations.
stories.source_signal_title: Asha Bhosle: The sound of Bollywood dies aged 92
body: Asha Bhosle has died aged 92, closing one of the most influential careers in Indian popular music. Her voice became inseparable from Bollywood's golden age and beyond, carrying songs that were heard across generations and far beyond India.

Bhosle was not simply a singer but part of the cultural machinery that shaped modern South Asian cinema. Her work helped define the emotional range of film music, from romantic ballads to lively dance numbers, and made her a familiar presence in homes, cinemas and public celebrations for decades.

Her death is likely to prompt an outpouring of tributes from across India and the wider diaspora, reflecting the rare cultural reach she achieved. It also marks the passing of an era in which playback singers could become national icons in their own right, with voices that became part of collective memory.

stories.title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
stories.summary: With tanker traffic still constrained and the ceasefire uncertain, markets are braced for another leg of pressure on oil, gas and transport costs.
stories.source_signal_title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
body: The collapse of US-Iran talks has left energy markets braced for a longer period of disruption rather than a swift return to normal. With tanker traffic still constrained and the ceasefire still uncertain, traders are now focused on how long shipments through the region remain impaired.

The Strait of Hormuz is central to global oil and gas flows, so even partial disruption can ripple through transport, refining and power costs. Higher freight rates and insurance premiums can feed into prices well beyond the Gulf, adding pressure to industries already facing tighter margins.

Consumers are likely to feel the effects more slowly, but they are unlikely to be spared if shipping remains subdued for long. Fuel costs can work through supply chains in stages, lifting the price of imported goods, food and travel before broader inflation measures fully reflect the shock.

What happens next depends on whether diplomacy resumes or the standoff hardens into a semi-permanent disruption. Markets are trying to judge not only the risk of another spike, but also whether this crisis resets a higher cost base for energy and transport over the months ahead.

stories.title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
stories.summary: The fuel shock is already feeding through into consumer prices, adding to the challenge for policymakers and households.
stories.source_signal_title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
body: Rising pump prices, driven by the Iran war, have pushed US inflation to its highest level in almost two years. The increase adds fresh pressure to households already contending with higher everyday costs and complicates the outlook for policymakers.

Fuel prices matter because they feed quickly into transport, delivery and production costs. Even where the direct impact on consumer prices is uneven, a sharp jump at the pump can alter inflation expectations and make it harder for central bankers to judge whether price pressures are temporary or persistent.

The latest figures are also a reminder that geopolitical shocks can reach domestic economies fast. If energy markets remain strained, the risk is that inflation eases more slowly than expected, keeping rates, wages and household budgets under sustained strain.

stories.title: EU airline industry warns of fuel shortages if Strait of Hormuz stays closed
stories.summary: Europe’s airlines are warning that jet fuel supply could tighten quickly if the waterway remains effectively shut.
stories.source_signal_title: EU airline industry warns of fuel shortages if Strait of Hormuz stays closed
body: Europe's airline industry has warned that jet fuel supply could tighten quickly if the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively shut. Airports Council International Europe says members are increasingly concerned about availability, a sign that the disruption is already moving beyond oil markets into aviation planning.

Airlines are especially exposed because fuel is one of their biggest costs and supply chains are finely balanced. If deliveries from the Gulf are delayed or rerouted, carriers may face higher prices, thinner margins and difficult choices over schedules and fares.

The warning matters because it shows how a shipping crisis can spread through the real economy. Even without a complete stoppage, prolonged uncertainty can force airlines and airports to build in contingency costs, which eventually tend to be passed on to passengers.

stories.title: Will UK petrol and diesel prices start going down?
stories.summary: A ceasefire may ease some pressure, but British drivers are unlikely to see an immediate or uniform fall in forecourt prices.
stories.source_signal_title: Will UK petrol and diesel prices start going down?
body: The ceasefire may ease some pressure on fuel markets, but British drivers should not expect an immediate or even uniform fall in petrol and diesel prices. Forecourt costs often move more slowly than global oil prices, because retailers and wholesalers adjust at different speeds.

The UK has already seen prices rise on the back of the Middle East conflict, and the effect can linger after conditions improve. Costs built up in shipping, refining and distribution do not disappear overnight, so the consumer impact can continue even if crude prices settle.

What happens next will depend on whether the truce holds and whether tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz returns to normal. If it does, prices should eventually ease; if not, drivers may remain stuck with a higher baseline for longer than they had hoped.

stories.title: How many ships are crossing the Strait of Hormuz?
stories.summary: A BBC Verify check suggests traffic through the strait remains far below normal, indicating how nervous shipping firms remain.
stories.source_signal_title: How many ships are crossing the Strait of Hormuz?
body: BBC Verify analysis suggests that only a few vessels have crossed the Strait of Hormuz since the ceasefire deal, far below normal traffic levels. That matters because the strait is one of the world's most important shipping routes, and a sharp drop in crossings is a clear sign that firms remain nervous.

Shipping companies are not waiting for a formal blockade before reacting. If insurance costs rise, crews face greater risk or the political situation looks unstable, firms can slow sailings, divert routes or hold vessels back altogether. The result is that a maritime crisis can deepen even when no ship has been stopped outright.

The low traffic levels indicate that confidence has not yet returned. Unless the security picture improves, the bottleneck could keep energy prices and freight costs elevated well beyond the immediate conflict.

stories.title: Interest in EVs surges in Europe as fuel prices jump after Iran war
stories.summary: Higher petrol costs are pushing more buyers towards electric cars, though demand may settle only after the crisis passes.
stories.source_signal_title: Interest in EVs surges in Europe as fuel prices jump after Iran war
body: Higher petrol prices are pushing more European buyers to look at electric cars as they reassess the cost of running a vehicle. The shift is being driven less by enthusiasm for technology than by a sudden reminder of how vulnerable drivers are to global fuel shocks.

The spike may not translate into a permanent change in demand, but it does expose how quickly consumer behaviour can change when fuel becomes more expensive. For manufacturers and dealers, that means a short-term lift in interest for EVs and hybrids, even if some of the demand later fades when prices settle.

The broader significance is that the energy crisis is affecting decisions well beyond oil markets. It is changing how households think about transport, and how governments and carmakers think about the pace of the transition away from petrol and diesel.

stories.title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
stories.summary: A Labour MP says public ownership may be the only workable outcome as talks over British Steel’s future continue.
stories.source_signal_title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
body: British Steel may need to be nationalised by the summer, according to Labour MP Nic Dakin, as talks over the company's future continue. The remarks reflect growing concern that the current ownership and investment model may not be stable enough to secure the plant's long-term survival.

British Steel remains strategically important because of the jobs it supports and the industrial capability it represents. Any move towards public ownership would be politically significant as well as economically costly, raising questions about how much state intervention ministers are willing to contemplate in order to protect domestic production.

The immediate issue is whether a private solution can be reached before the business deteriorates further. If it cannot, pressure will build on the government to act, even if nationalisation is framed as a temporary measure rather than a permanent policy shift.

stories.title: OpenAI pauses UK data centre deal over energy costs and regulation
stories.summary: The project’s pause is a reminder that expensive power and regulatory uncertainty can still blunt Britain’s ambitions to attract AI investment.
stories.source_signal_title: OpenAI pauses UK data centre deal over energy costs and regulation
body: OpenAI has paused a UK data centre deal, highlighting the practical limits of Britain's efforts to attract large-scale AI investment. Energy costs and regulatory uncertainty are once again proving to be the sort of obstacles that can slow even high-profile technology projects.

The pause matters because data centres are extremely power-intensive and need both cheap electricity and a predictable planning environment. If either becomes expensive or uncertain, projects can be delayed, scaled back or shifted elsewhere, despite the political enthusiasm for positioning the UK as an AI hub.

For ministers, the episode is a warning that rhetoric about becoming an AI superpower is not enough. Investors still want affordable power, clear rules and faster decisions, and without them the UK's competitive advantage may remain more aspirational than real.

stories.title: Faisal Islam: Why the government is relaxed about Chinese car imports
stories.summary: The government appears willing to accept cheaper Chinese vehicles as part of a wider calculation about consumers, competition and industrial policy.
stories.source_signal_title: Faisal Islam: Why the government is relaxed about Chinese car imports
body: The government's apparent calm about Chinese car imports reflects a wider calculation about consumers, competition and industrial policy. Cheaper vehicles can help keep prices down at a time when households are under pressure, even if they also unsettle domestic manufacturers.

Ministers seem to accept that the rise of China's car industry cannot simply be wished away. Instead, they appear to be betting that competition will force innovation and make the market more affordable, while Britain tries to protect the parts of its industrial base it still considers strategically important.

That approach carries risks. Greater imports can ease short-term pressures for drivers, but they may also deepen anxiety about jobs, supply chains and state support for British industry. The government's stance suggests it is prioritising flexibility over outright protection.

stories.title: Tice £91,000 tax row is 'minor administrative error', party claims
stories.summary: The dispute has political as well as financial implications, given the scrutiny now falling on Reform UK’s leadership and finances.
stories.source_signal_title: Tice £91,000 tax row is 'minor administrative error', party claims
body: Reform UK is describing the £91,000 tax dispute involving its deputy leader as a minor administrative error, but the issue carries more than financial significance. At a moment when the party is trying to present itself as a serious national force, scrutiny over its finances and leadership judgement is politically awkward.

The allegation, reported by the Sunday Times, concerns a property company owned by Richard Tice and unpaid tax on dividends. While the party is rejecting the implication of wrongdoing, the row is likely to fuel questions about governance, transparency and standards within the organisation.

The wider test is not simply whether the sum is eventually settled, but how convincingly the party handles the controversy. In an election climate, tax and conduct disputes can quickly become proxies for whether voters trust a party to run institutions responsibly.

stories.title: Starmer urges US and Iran to 'find way through' after peace talks falter
stories.summary: The prime minister is trying to keep Britain outside the direct line of fire while urging restraint after the failed talks.
stories.source_signal_title: Starmer urges US and Iran to 'find way through' after peace talks falter
body: Sir Keir Starmer is urging the US and Iran to find a way through after peace talks failed to produce an agreement. His aim is to keep Britain out of the direct line of fire while still backing restraint, a familiar balancing act when a Middle East crisis threatens to widen.

The prime minister's warning against escalation reflects the limited room available to London. Britain has influence, but not control, over events in Washington or Tehran, and its immediate interest is to avoid a confrontation that could drag in British interests, shipping and energy supplies.

What happens next depends on whether the ceasefire can be preserved long enough for diplomacy to restart. If talks resume, Starmer and other leaders will likely present them as the only credible route back from the brink; if not, the pressure on Britain to respond to events may quickly intensify.

stories.title: Britain could adopt single market rules without MPs’ vote as part of UK-EU reset
stories.summary: Ministers are preparing a potentially significant shift in the UK-EU relationship, with parliamentary scrutiny likely to become a major flashpoint.
stories.source_signal_title: Britain could adopt single market rules without MPs’ vote as part of UK-EU reset
body: Ministers are considering a significant shift in the UK's relationship with the European Union, with Britain potentially adopting some single market rules without a vote in the Commons. That would mark a notable step in the government's wider effort to reset ties with Brussels after years of post-Brexit friction.

The issue is politically sensitive because it goes to the heart of sovereignty and parliamentary scrutiny. Supporters of the approach may argue that closer alignment would reduce trade barriers and practical frictions, while critics will say it hands too much influence to EU rules without enough democratic oversight.

If the plan advances, the main flashpoint is likely to be Parliament itself. Ministers may see regulatory alignment as a pragmatic way to improve trade, but they will still need to manage accusations that they are restoring EU influence by the back door.

stories.title: Home Office to announce closure of 11 asylum hotels in next week
stories.summary: The closures would mark a modest step towards Labour’s pledge to end the use of hotels for asylum accommodation by the end of this parliament.
stories.source_signal_title: Home Office to announce closure of 11 asylum hotels in next week
body: The Home Office is expected to announce the closure of 11 asylum hotels next week, a modest but politically important step in Labour's pledge to end the use of hotels for asylum accommodation by the end of this parliament. The policy remains under intense scrutiny because of cost, pressure on local services and public opposition in some areas.

Closing a limited number of hotels will not by itself solve the wider asylum accommodation backlog. But it may allow ministers to argue that they are starting to reduce reliance on emergency housing while they look for more stable and cheaper alternatives.

The larger question is whether the government can cut hotel use without simply shifting pressure elsewhere in the system. That depends on how quickly asylum claims are processed, how many people can be moved into other accommodation and whether the promised reforms deliver in practice.

stories.title: Deep-fried food banned in new plans for school dinners
stories.summary: A wider school food overhaul signals a more interventionist approach to childhood obesity and nutrition standards.
stories.source_signal_title: Deep-fried food banned in new plans for school dinners
body: New plans for school dinners would ban deep-fried food, in a sign that ministers are taking a more interventionist approach to childhood nutrition. The changes are part of a wider effort to reduce sugar, increase vegetables and whole grains, and improve the quality of food served in schools.

Supporters will argue that schools have a duty to help shape healthier habits, especially where diet-related illness is a long-term concern. Critics may see the move as another example of the state reaching too far into family life and school autonomy.

The practical challenge is to make healthier meals appealing, affordable and workable for schools already under budget pressure. If the plans are to succeed, they will need to improve nutrition without simply generating waste or resentment among pupils and caterers.

stories.title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
stories.summary: As public scepticism rises, the biggest AI firms are leaning harder on policy papers, lobbying and institutional influence.
stories.source_signal_title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
body: The biggest AI companies increasingly know they have an image problem. As public scepticism rises, they are leaning harder on policy papers, thinktanks and institutional influence in an attempt to shape how governments and the public think about the technology.

That strategy reflects a recognition that technical progress alone does not guarantee trust. AI firms are now trying to present themselves as responsible actors with answers on jobs, safety and regulation, while also pushing for a policy environment that is favourable to their business models.

The concern is that this looks less like a public conversation and more like a contest over agenda-setting. If AI companies fund the debates that are meant to scrutinise them, the result may be more influence, but not necessarily more credibility.

What happens next will depend on whether policymakers treat these interventions as useful expertise or as reputational management. As AI becomes more powerful, the struggle over who gets to define the risks may matter almost as much as the technology itself.

stories.title: ‘Too powerful for the public’: Inside Anthropic’s bid to win the AI publicity war
stories.summary: Anthropic’s claims about a withheld model raise familiar questions about safety, marketing and the politics of AI credibility.
stories.source_signal_title: ‘Too powerful for the public’: Inside Anthropic’s bid to win the AI publicity war
body: Anthropic's claims about a model too powerful to release have put safety and marketing back at the centre of the AI debate. The company is presenting itself as more cautious than some rivals, but the message also functions as part of a wider competition for credibility in a crowded market.

The episode matters because AI companies are no longer competing only on technical performance. They are also competing on trust, trying to persuade regulators, journalists and customers that their systems are advanced enough to matter but controlled enough not to alarm the public.

That is a difficult line to hold. If a company stresses danger too strongly, it risks looking opportunistic or evasive; if it downplays the risk, it can seem reckless. Anthropic's challenge is to show that its warnings are a genuine safety stance rather than a branding exercise.

stories.title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
stories.summary: Brain-computer and avatar technology is giving a performer with motor neurone disease a new way to appear on stage.
stories.source_signal_title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
body: A dancer living with motor neurone disease has performed on stage again through a digital avatar, in a striking example of how brain-computer and avatar technology is changing what is possible for performers with severe physical limitations. The technology allowed her to reappear in a form closer to the artistic expression she remembered.

The significance goes beyond one performance. As assistive technologies improve, they are opening up new ways for people with degenerative illnesses to communicate, create and participate in public life, even when the body can no longer do so in the usual way.

There are still obvious limits: the technology cannot reverse the disease, and it remains expensive and complex. But it offers a glimpse of a future in which digital tools can preserve agency and presence in ways that were previously unimaginable.

stories.title: Meta pulls Facebook ads recruiting for social media addiction lawsuits
stories.summary: The company is trying to contain litigation risk after a landmark social media addiction case in California.
stories.source_signal_title: Meta pulls Facebook ads recruiting for social media addiction lawsuits
body: Meta has pulled Facebook ads recruiting people for social media addiction lawsuits, as it seeks to limit litigation risk after a landmark California case. The move suggests the company is treating the legal and reputational stakes of addiction claims as increasingly serious.

The issue is sensitive because it goes to the heart of how platforms make money. If courts and regulators accept arguments that social media can fuel harmful compulsive use, companies like Meta could face more lawsuits, tougher oversight and greater pressure to redesign parts of their products.

For now, the company appears to be trying to contain exposure rather than confront the broader criticism head-on. But as more evidence and lawsuits emerge, the dispute is likely to remain central to the debate over the responsibilities of major tech platforms.

stories.title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
stories.summary: Wout van Aert won a classic battle with Tadej Pogacar to take one of cycling’s biggest one-day races.
stories.source_signal_title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
body: Wout van Aert beat Tadej Pogacar in a thrilling sprint finish to win Paris-Roubaix, after one of the most compelling editions of cycling's toughest one-day race. The result underlined Van Aert's resilience and gave him a victory that will sit high among his biggest classics wins.

Pogacar, a four-time Tour de France champion, was central to the drama throughout the race, helping turn it into a rare head-to-head battle between two of the sport's biggest names. The contest mattered because it reminded fans how powerful a clash of elite riders can be in a race built on exhaustion, dust and brutality.

Paris-Roubaix remains one of cycling's great tests because it punishes any weakness in positioning, timing or endurance. Winning it requires not just strength but nerve, and Van Aert's sprint finish showed he had both when it mattered most.

stories.title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals
stories.summary: City’s win over Chelsea has tightened the title race and set up a decisive run-in at the top of the Premier League.
stories.source_signal_title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals
body: Manchester City's win over Chelsea has tightened the Premier League title race and given Pep Guardiola's side momentum at exactly the right moment. Arsenal's slip-up has opened the door, and City now look like a team sensing opportunity rather than simply waiting for one.

The significance lies in the timing. With the run-in approaching, City have more room for error on paper because of their game in hand, but they also have the psychological edge of having put pressure straight back on the leaders.

Arsenal's concern is not only City's points total but their habit of capitalising ruthlessly on uncertainty. If City keep winning and Arsenal falter again, the title race could turn quickly from competitive to deeply uncomfortable for Mikel Arteta's side.

stories.title: Will Romero's tears become the enduring image of Tottenham's season?
stories.summary: Tottenham’s defeat at Sunderland deepens their crisis and raises the prospect of a season-defining collapse.
stories.source_signal_title: Will Romero's tears become the enduring image of Tottenham's season?
body: Cristian Romero's tears after Tottenham's defeat at Sunderland may come to symbolise a season slipping beyond control. The loss leaves Spurs in the relegation zone and deepens the sense that the club is in a crisis that cannot be explained away as a bad afternoon.

The result is especially damaging because it came in Roberto De Zerbi's first game in charge. A new manager usually brings at least a brief lift, but Tottenham instead looked fragile and short of the authority needed to climb out of trouble.

If the season does collapse, Romero's reaction could become the image supporters remember most. It captured not just disappointment but the sense of a club under intense strain, with little certainty about how quickly its form can be rescued.

stories.title: Sinner beats Alcaraz to return to world top spot
stories.summary: Jannik Sinner’s win in Monte Carlo restores him to world number one, underlining the intensity of the men’s tennis rivalry.
stories.source_signal_title: Sinner beats Alcaraz to return to world top spot
body: Jannik Sinner has returned to world number one after beating Carlos Alcaraz in the Monte Carlo final. The result adds another chapter to a rivalry that is increasingly shaping men's tennis and suggests the balance at the top may keep shifting.

Sinner's victory matters because it shows he can handle one of the sport's most demanding match-ups when a ranking and a title are both on the line. Alcaraz remains his closest rival, and the back-and-forth between them has become central to the current men's game.

For tennis, the contest is valuable because it gives the tour a genuine rivalry with style, stakes and momentum. For both players, the challenge now is consistency: staying at the top in a sport where form can change quickly from one tournament to the next.
